title: Relevant Segment Extraction (RSE)
one_liner: Post-retrieval step that reconstructs contiguous multi-chunk document segments around clusters of relevant chunks, so the LLM sees complete in-order sections instead of isolated top-k fragments.
problem_solved: Fixed-size chunking forces a bad tradeoff: small chunks retrieve precisely but strip surrounding context, while large chunks carry context but retrieve poorly. Top-k retrieval also returns chunks out of document order and misses chunks that are essential but individually score as irrelevant (e.g. the middle rows of a financial statement sandwiched between high-scoring chunks). RSE dynamically sizes the returned context per query by finding contiguous spans of high-relevance chunks and returning them whole and in order.
failure_modes: Retrieved chunks lack the surrounding context needed to actually answer the question
Answers that span a whole section or table get fragmented because only a few of its chunks score as relevant
Chunks arrive at the LLM out of document order, breaking the flow of multi-chunk content like financial statements or legal arguments
One fixed chunk size can't serve both single-sentence factoid queries and broad section-level queries
Important 'connector' chunks sandwiched between relevant chunks are dropped because the reranker scores them low individually
Top-k retrieval wastes context budget on scattered isolated chunks instead of one coherent passage
when_to_use: Your documents are long reports (10-Ks, annual reports, academic papers, legal opinions) where facts depend on surrounding sections
Queries range from narrow factoids to broad 'summarize/explain the whole section' questions against the same corpus
You observe that relevant chunks cluster together in source documents (plot chunk relevance by index to check)
You already run a reranker and want a cheap (~5-10ms, no extra LLM calls) post-processing step with large measured accuracy gains
Multi-chunk structured content like consolidated financial statements or multi-page tables must be presented intact
You control the indexing pipeline and can chunk with zero overlap and store chunks keyed by doc_id + chunk_index
tradeoffs: Requires chunking with no overlap so segments can be reconstructed by concatenation — incompatible with overlapping-chunk indexes without re-indexing
Needs a key-value store (doc_id, chunk_index -> text) alongside the vector DB, since segments include chunks never returned by search
Works best with a reranker (Cohere Rerank here), adding a paid API dependency and per-query rerank latency/cost
The notebook's brute-force max-subarray search is demo-grade; production needs the more sophisticated dsRAG implementation
Segments can overshoot the ideal boundary and include bonus chunks you don't need, spending some context budget
Several tunables (irrelevant_chunk_penalty ~0.2, decay_rate, max_length, overall_max_length, minimum_value) need empirical tuning per corpus
No benefit for corpora of short independent documents (FAQs, tickets) where relevant chunks don't cluster — it just reduces to top-k
Notebook simplifies by reranking all 500 chunks of one document directly; at corpus scale you still need vector/keyword search to narrow candidates first
how_it_works: Operates after vector search and reranking, before generation (composes with chunking constraints at indexing time). 1) Documents are split with RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter at chunk_size=800 and chunk_overlap=0 so contiguous chunks can be concatenated back into segments. 2) Cohere Rerank (rerank-english-v3.0) scores every candidate chunk against the query; scores are spread out with a beta CDF transform (a=b=0.4) because raw reranker scores bunch near 0 and 1. 3) Each chunk's value fuses absolute relevance with relevance rank via exponential decay: chunk_value = exp(-rank/decay_rate) * transformed_score, which is more robust than score or rank alone. 4) A constant irrelevant_chunk_penalty (~0.2) is subtracted so irrelevant chunks go negative; segment value is then simply the sum of its chunk values, turning segment selection into a constrained maximum-sum-subarray problem. 5) get_best_segments greedily brute-force searches for non-overlapping (start, end) spans subject to max_length per segment, overall_max_length across segments, and a minimum_value threshold, skipping negative-valued endpoints (~5-10ms). Selected segments are returned in original document order; when relevance is isolated rather than clustered, the method degrades gracefully to standard top-k. Evals: KITE average 4.72 -> 6.73 vs top-k=20; combined with contextual chunk headers, 83% vs 19% baseline on FinanceBench.
key_code_signals: RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=800, chunk_overlap=0) — zero-overlap chunking so segments concatenate cleanly
beta.cdf(x, 0.4, 0.4) transform to spread clustered reranker scores toward uniform
chunk_values[index] = np.exp(-i/decay_rate) * absolute_relevance_value — fusion of rank decay and similarity score
relevance_values = [v - irrelevant_chunk_penalty for v in chunk_values] — thresholding so irrelevant chunks go negative
get_best_segments(relevance_values, max_length, overall_max_length, minimum_value) — constrained maximum-sum-subarray optimization returning (start, end) spans
plot_relevance_scores() scatter of chunk relevance vs chunk index to reveal clustering
related_techniques: context_enrichment_window_around_chunk: alternative — fixed-size neighbor windows vs RSE's query-adaptive optimized segments
contextual_chunk_headers: composes — evaluated jointly with RSE on FinanceBench (83% vs 19% baseline)
reranking: prerequisite — RSE consumes reranker relevance scores as its input signal
choose_chunk_size: refines — RSE dissolves the fixed-chunk-size tradeoff by sizing context dynamically per query
semantic_chunking: alternative — attacks the same context-fragmentation problem at chunking time instead of post-retrieval
contextual_compression: complementary-opposite — compression shrinks retrieved context, RSE expands it to complete segments
dsRAG (external library): production implementation of RSE referenced by the notebook
summary: Relevant Segment Extraction (RSE) is a post-retrieval step that reconstructs contiguous multi-chunk segments from source documents instead of feeding isolated, out-of-order top-k chunks to the LLM. It exploits the observation that relevant chunks cluster within documents. After search and reranking, each chunk gets a value fusing its Cohere reranker score (spread via a beta CDF transform) with an exponential rank decay; subtracting a constant irrelevant_chunk_penalty (~0.2) makes irrelevant chunks negative, so a segment's value is the sum of its chunk values and finding optimal segments becomes a constrained maximum-sum-subarray problem solved by brute force in ~5-10ms with no extra LLM calls. This recovers 'sandwiched' chunks the reranker scored low, presents text in original document order, and adapts context size per query — short for factoids, whole sections for broad questions — degrading gracefully to top-k when relevance is isolated. Requires zero-overlap chunking and a doc_id/chunk_index key-value store. KITE benchmark: 4.72 to 6.73 average vs top-k=20; with contextual chunk headers, 83% on FinanceBench vs 19% baseline. Production implementation lives in the dsRAG library.
notes_for_chunking: High-quality notebook for chunking: strong markdown headers (Overview, Motivation, Key Components, Method Details, Setup, chunk-relevance visualization, segment optimization, Eval results) with roughly 50/50 markdown/code ratio and unusually explanatory markdown — the conceptual cells (cell-1, cell-19, cell-22, cell-23) are self-contained and embed well on their own. Code cells carry detailed docstrings that restate intent, so code+preceding-markdown pairs make natural chunks. Junk to strip: leading book-promotion cell (cell-0), pip-install cell (cell-5), wget data-download cell (cell-9), the view-tracker image cell at the end (cell-24), and two large matplotlib base64 image outputs (cells 13 and 15). The plot_relevance_scores and print_document_segment helpers are boilerplate visualization, low retrieval value. The Eval results section (KITE tables, FinanceBench numbers) is a distinct self-contained unit worth keeping as its own chunk. Interleaved output cells (segment indices/scores) are small and informative, safe to keep inline.
